supply_chain.dependencies: Natural gas supply from various suppliers including Piedmont; uranium concentrates, conversion services, enrichment services from diversified long-term contracts; coal from long-term contracts with mining operators; single domestic supplier for fuel fabrication services on plant-by-plant basis
risks: Supply chain challenges for major equipment components for new generation and grid infrastructure
Supply chain disruptions from extreme weather events

Concentration risk in fuel fabrication services (single domestic supplier per plant); reliance on long-term coal contracts expiring 2025-2030; uranium supply contracts covering requirements through 2027-2034; natural gas transportation and storage dependencies; technical complexities of changing fuel fabrication suppliers
Fluctuations in commodity prices or availability may adversely affect operations
Inability to build additional natural gas infrastructure at a pace that meets demand could limit growth opportunities
Concentration of coal ash handling system suppliers

Significant disruption to interstate pipeline capacity or reduction in natural gas supply due to operational failures, hurricanes, tornadoes, floods, freeze-off of natural gas wells, terrorist or cyberattacks, or legislative/regulatory actions
Equipment delivery delays impacting capital projects
Disruption in fuel delivery due to economic conditions, bankruptcies, transportation delays, weather, labor relations, force majeure events, or environmental regulations
Potential impacts from new tariffs or other actions that could disrupt Duke Energy's supply chain
Commodity price volatility for fuel and purchased power

regulatory_risks: Federal and state regulations designed to promote energy efficiency measures, natural gas electrification, and distributed generation technologies
Compliance with environmental laws may result in fines, penalties, injunctive measures, or facility shutdowns
PHMSA regulations on natural gas distribution and transmission system safety and security
Fuel cost adjustment clause mechanisms and prudence reviews
State utility commission regulations (NCUC, PSCSC, FPSC, IURC, PUCO, KPSC, TPUC)
Rate regulation by state utility commissions (NCUC, PSCSC, FPSC, IURC, PUCO, KPSC, TPUC) and FERC
Deregulation or restructuring in the electric industry may result in increased competition and unrecovered costs
Nuclear facility licensing and regulatory compliance with NRC
State utility commission decisions on cost recovery for coal ash closure, storm costs, and other expenditures
NRC requirements for nuclear operating license renewals
Potential local government initiatives to eliminate natural gas use in new buildings
Storm cost recovery and securitization regulatory approvals
Transmission cost allocation and FERC jurisdiction
Regulatory restrictions on dividend payments and fund transfers from subsidiaries to Duke Energy
Regulatory delays or failure to obtain environmental permits for operating assets or development projects
Uncertainty of applicability or changes to legislative and regulatory initiatives related to climate change
State-level climate change legislation and carbon reduction goals more aggressive than company plans
NRC regulations for nuclear facility operations
North Carolina Coal Ash Management Act of 2014 compliance
State and federal regulations on methane emissions from natural gas distribution
Climate change and carbon emissions regulations
Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) compliance
Environmental regulations including EPA rules on coal combustion residuals (CCR)
Potential legal challenges to EPA rules and other environmental regulations
Pipeline integrity management compliance with federal regulations
Potential changes to tax incentives and credits under the Inflation Reduction Act
Costs of decommissioning nuclear facilities may not be fully recoverable through the regulatory process
2024 EPA CCR Rule imposing stringent GHG emission reduction standards, revised air toxic limits, and wastewater discharge limitations
EPA regulations on greenhouse gas emissions, air quality, water quality, and coal combustion residuals may increase compliance costs
Failure to obtain regulatory approvals for construction projects or rate increases
State utility commissions' authority over rates, construction, and operation of facilities may limit ability to recover costs
Changes in rules for regional transmission organizations, including changes in rate designs and new capacity markets
Tax law changes including Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) impacts
Renewable energy mandates and clean energy transition requirements
Rulings that affect cost and investment recovery or have an impact on rate structures or market prices
Potential disallowance of costs by regulatory commissions, including fuel costs, storm costs, and coal ash closure costs
State, federal and foreign legislative and regulatory initiatives, including costs of compliance with existing and future environmental requirements
Federal and state laws, regulations and legal requirements related to coal ash remediation
North Carolina Coal Ash Management Act requirements for closure and remediation of coal ash basins
NRC regulations for nuclear facility design, construction, operation, and decommissioning
Depreciation rate changes and asset retirement obligation recovery
Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN) requirements for new generation
EPA Rule 111 requiring existing coal-fired plants to reduce GHG emissions by 90% through carbon capture starting in 2032
FERC regulations for wholesale power sales and transmission
Securities and financial reporting regulations
Coal ash basin closure requirements under federal and state regulations
EPA regulations including 2015 CCR Rule and 2024 CCR Rule for coal combustion residuals
FERC regulations affecting wholesale electric sales, transmission services, and natural gas pipeline certification
